local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council approved key contracts and dropped potential tree litigation during its April 7 business meeting. The council unanimously approved a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering for gasification system commissioning at the wastewater treatment plant, eliminated two unfilled wastewater operator positions while adding two supervisor positions, and approved a $419,000 sewer pipe rehabilitation contract covering 27 locations. The council also declined to pursue a counterpetition in the Redelfs tree removal case, where a 33-inch-diameter landmark tree was removed without permit, with the fine reduced from $102,245 to $20,000 by the hearing examiner. Additionally, the council declared April 9 as Local News Day and approved budget amendments including advancing funds for the Edm [TRUNCATED]

The City of Edmonds will begin construction on its 2025 sewer and storm pipe rehabilitation project starting May 4. The Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project will repair aging sewer and storm pipes using trenchless technology to minimize disruption. This infrastructure improvement project addresses critical maintenance needs identified in previous system assessments and represents ongoing investment in the city's utility infrastructure.

The City of Edmonds has reached the 60% design level for the Highway 99 Revitalization Project - Stage 3 and is seeking public input through an Urban Design Elements survey. The project aims to revitalize the southern half-mile stretch of Highway 99 from 244th St SW to 238th St SW, improving safety for all transportation users while preserving the area's diverse character that includes local businesses, family homes, and modern industry. The survey, which closes May 14, will help shape final design decisions for urban design elements along the corridor.

South County Fire Board of Commissioners is actively working on redistricting proposals to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority's five commissioner districts. Two alternative plans have emerged: Plan 1 would place nearly all of Edmonds into a single district, while Plan 2 would split the city along Pine Street between two districts. The board is working with consultant Robert Schweitzer and will hold public hearings to gather community input before finalizing district boundaries that must comply with equal population, compactness, and contiguity requirements under state law.

Public safety and environmental issues dominated the April 28 Edmonds City Council meeting, with councilmembers receiving annual reports from organizations providing public safety services to Edmonds. The council focused on reviewing public safety performance metrics and environmental concerns affecting community safety, though specific incident details were not provided in the available coverage.

career.notes: Most districts use pooled postings for secondary English and History rather than single-school listings. Apply to every relevant pool and monitor district sites weekly for specific openings. Salaries listed where disclosed; otherwise, refer to each district’s published salary schedule. All positions require Washington State teaching certificate.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded their 2025-26 season auditions, but auditions for the 2026-27 season will be held next August. To be notified when scheduling opens, email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join their auditions mailing list. The choir rehearses Tuesday evenings 6:30-9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave, Seattle) and performs three major concerts each season plus outreach events. Requirements: music-reading skills, prior singing or instrumental experience, and familiarity with foreign languages. Annual dues are $75 with scholarships available. The 80-voice ensemble also maintains three small ensembles (Vox, Chroma, Orpheon) that audition from within the full choir after the season begins.
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offers weekly Toddler Storytimes perfect for 2-year-olds in May. Edmonds Library hosts Toddler Storytime with Cami every Thursday at 9:00-9:45 am. Lynnwood Library offers Toddler Storytime with Nathalie every Monday at 10:30-11:00 am. Both programs feature stories, songs, rhymes and activities designed to build early literacy skills. The libraries also provide Baby Storytime (0-18 months) and Preschool Storytime (3-5 years) at various branches. All programs are free and no registration is required. Check sno-isle.org for complete schedules and any holiday closures.

global_news.findings: Oil prices surged to a new wartime high above $126 per barrel Thursday as President Trump considers extending the naval blockade of Iranian ports. The US-Iran conflict has now entered its ninth week with no clear resolution, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified before Congress that critics of the war are "the biggest adversary." Iran's oil minister urged citizens to cut consumption amid the ongoing crisis.

President Trump stated at a White House state dinner for King Charles that Britain's monarch agrees Iran should never have nuclear weapons. The comment came during Charles' historic four-day US state visit, which has been marked by tensions over the Middle East conflict and NATO relations. Trump emphasized that "Charles agrees with me even more than I do" on preventing Iran from obtaining nuclear capabilities.

Ukraine has accused Israel of receiving grain shipments that were "stolen" by Russia from occupied Ukrainian territory. President Zelensky warned that Israeli authorities "cannot be unaware" of the cargo origins and stated this undermines bilateral relations. Israel's Foreign Minister rejected what he called "Twitter diplomacy" and demanded evidence through proper diplomatic channels.

The United States and Iran clashed at the United Nations over Tehran's selection as a vice president for the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty conference. The US called Iran's appointment an "affront" to the NPT's credibility, while Iran accused Washington of hypocrisy given its own nuclear arsenal. The confrontation highlights the ongoing diplomatic deadlock between the two nations.

King Charles III and Queen Camilla began their historic four-day US state visit with a military welcome, as the monarch prepares to address Congress on defending "democratic values." The visit comes amid heightened tensions following the assassination attempt on President Trump and ongoing debates about transatlantic relations and NATO commitments.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica/FRONTLINE investigation reveals that more than 300 U.S. citizens were arrested during Trump-era immigration sweeps on charges such as assaulting or interfering with federal agents. Yet, in over a third of the cases, prosecutors quietly dismissed the charges after video evidence contradicted officers’ sworn statements. The pattern—high-profile raids followed by collapsed prosecutions—has created what legal scholars call a “chilling pre-conviction effect,” where the mere fact of arrest deters future protest regardless of ultimate guilt. The report centers on Alejandro Orell [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy argues that literature’s value lies not in moral improvement but in its capacity to keep past sensations alive. Drawing on Proust’s 1905 essay “On Reading”—written as a preface to Ruskin’s Sesame and Lilies—she shows how Proust rejected Ruskin’s utilitarian view of books as tools for social advancement. Instead, Proust claimed that reading is a unique form of communication that preserves the texture of lost time, allowing us to “grasp time without abolishing it.” Champy extends this to defend the disinterested act of reading as an antidote to a culture obsessed with measurable out [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova revisits the life of Frances “Fanny” Wright (1795–1852), the Scottish-born American reformer who fused Epicurean philosophy, scientific empiricism, and radical abolitionism. At eighteen Wright composed A Few Days in Athens, a fictional Greek manuscript that championed atomic realism and moral discipline; later she founded the Nashoba commune in Tennessee to test interracial coexistence and free love. Popova frames Wright as a missing link between Enlightenment rationalism and Whitmanic democracy—Walt Whitman himself recalled that she “possessed my body and soul”—and argues that Wr [TRUNCATED]

In a second major feature, ProPublica details how the Trump administration has systematically replaced career election-security staff with figures from the 2020 election-denial movement. At least 75 federal employees have been reassigned or forced out, while two dozen new appointees—ten of whom participated in attempts to overturn the 2020 vote—now occupy key positions at CISA, the Justice Department, and the FBI. The story reconstructs a December 2020 meeting in which Attorney General William Barr, after hearing technical evidence that vote-hacking claims were baseless, likened his impending  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Christopher Isabelle's "Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field" (2026) advances a radical claim: dyadic structures—self/world, subject/object, true/false—are necessary but insufficient for emergence and meaning. A third term, the relational field, must be actively maintained through tension between poles or intelligibility itself disappears. Language compresses this triadic structure and cannot reconstruct its origin. The new Karl Alber series "Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy" launches with Band 1 "Trinitarian Ontologies: Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy" (2025), collecting contributions from Münster and Prague that bridge analytic and continental approaches. In quantum foundations, the March 2026 Springer paper "Symmetric and asymmetric tripartite states under the lens of entanglement splitting and topological linking" establishes a direct operational link between three-qubit entanglement and topological lin [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 papers move autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. Google Cloud's MARS framework (arXiv:2602.02660) introduces Budget-Aware Planning via cost-constrained Monte-Carlo Tree Search, letting agents trade off model-training cost against expected performance gain; on MLE-Bench it now sits at the top of the open-source leaderboard while spending 37 % less GPU time than last year’s best. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) couples generation, verification and evolution subsystems so a single agent can run both computational modelling and wet-lab experiments in a unified loop; it has already autonomously re-derived a competitive optimiser for low-rank matrix completion and is being stress-tested on a 30-day continuous climate-data discovery run. MiroFlow (arXiv:2602.22808) wraps these advances in an open-source orchestration graph that supports GAIA, HLE and FutureX benchmarks out of the box, giving the community a reproducible baseline. On the prompting si [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come rea [TRUNCATED]

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: A Pulitzer Prize-winning debut novel that reimagines the antebellum South through the story of Henry Townsend, a Black farmer and former slave who becomes a slave owner himself. Set in Manchester County, Virginia, the novel explores the moral complexities of slavery, freedom, and identity, challenging conventional narratives about race and power in American history. Through a rich tapestry of characters and interwoven stories, Jones crafts a profound meditation on the human condition and the enduring legacy of slavery.

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom
enriched_articles.text:
# The role of literature as the key to personal freedom | Aeon Essays

Published: 2026-04-24T10:00:00+00:00

## Summary

Marcel Proust, the author of his novel, In Search of Lost Time (1905), has defended the importance of literature as a key to personal freedom and its ability to influence the lives of individuals. He argues that books are not always the instruments of moral betterment, but they can provide valuable insights into our lives. This comes after John Ruskin argued that books were no
